description: Architecture decision rules for /architect skill. Each rule encodes an overengineering trap or tech-debt pattern with a condition (trigger), recommendation (safe default), dont_use list (what to avoid), and a test_scenario for validation.

rules.name: sql_first
rules.condition: Choosing a database for a new project with < 100M rows, < 1TB data, or < 1000 ops/sec
rules.recommendation: Use a single PostgreSQL instance (or SQLite for tiny apps). A well-tuned SQL DB on one server handles millions of records. Scale vertically first, then read-replicas. Use JSONB columns for schemaless parts.
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev building a personal finance app with <100k users asks what database to use. Rule should recommend PostgreSQL, not MongoDB.

rules.name: local_vector_store
rules.condition: Building RAG or semantic search with < 100K embeddings or < 1GB text corpus
rules.recommendation: Use a local vector store: pgvector (if already using Postgres), FAISS, LanceDB, or Chroma locally. In-memory search is faster and simpler. Only move to hosted vector DB when data exceeds single-machine capacity or you need multi-user concurrent high-QPS.
rules.test_scenario: Developer building a personal notes chatbot with 5K documents asks about vector databases. Rule should recommend FAISS or pgvector, not Pinecone.

rules.name: modular_monolith
rules.condition: Starting a new project with a single developer or small team (< 5 people)
rules.recommendation: Build a modular monolith — one deployable unit with clear internal module boundaries. Function calls over network calls. Structure code into modules/folders by domain. Only split into services when you feel real scaling or team-coordination pain. Martin Fowler: almost all successful microservices started as a monolith.
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev asks whether to use microservices for a new SaaS MVP. Rule should recommend a modular monolith, not microservices.

rules.name: simple_api
rules.condition: Building an API with one or two known client types (e.g., your own web frontend + maybe a mobile app)
rules.recommendation: Use simple RESTful JSON endpoints. Tailor responses to what the UI needs. Use OpenAPI for docs if needed. Only adopt GraphQL when you have multiple independent consumers with diverse data-fetch patterns. Only use gRPC for high-performance inter-service comms at proven scale.
dont_use: API Gateway products (for a handful of endpoints)
BFF micro-layer (when one dev controls both ends)
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev building a React app with a Python backend asks whether to use GraphQL. Rule should recommend REST, since they control both client and server.

rules.name: simple_deployment
rules.condition: Deploying an app with low-to-moderate traffic (< 10K concurrent users) as a single developer
rules.recommendation: Use a single VPS ($5-10/month) or PaaS (Heroku, Fly.io, Render, Vercel, Cloud Run). Docker Compose on one node if you want containers. Process manager (PM2, Gunicorn, systemd) is enough. Only consider K8s when you have multiple services and manual management breaks down.
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev asks how to deploy a Python web app + database. Rule should recommend Cloud Run or a single VPS, not Kubernetes.

rules.name: simple_auth
rules.condition: Building auth for an app with < 1000 users or a handful of known internal users
rules.recommendation: Use managed auth (Firebase Auth, Supabase Auth, Auth0 free tier) or your framework's built-in auth (Django auth, Rails Devise). For internal tools, Google OAuth or even HTTP Basic behind a proxy may suffice. Only deploy enterprise IAM (Keycloak, Okta custom) when you have multi-app SSO needs or compliance requirements.
dont_use: custom OAuth2 authorization code flow between own services
dedicated RBAC microservice (for simple role checks)
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev building a SaaS with email/password login and Google sign-in for ~50 beta users. Rule should recommend Firebase Auth or Supabase, not Keycloak.

rules.name: direct_calls_over_queues
rules.condition: Task flow with low throughput (< 100 tasks/minute) or simple async needs in a single-service app
rules.recommendation: Use direct function calls or lightweight scheduling. For background tasks, use framework job queues (Celery, Sidekiq) or cron jobs. For simple delayed work, use in-memory task queues or database-backed job tables. Only use dedicated message brokers when you have multiple independent consumers, high-volume producers, or true fire-hose data streams.
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev needs to send confirmation emails after orders (10 orders/day). Rule should recommend a simple background function or cron, not Kafka.

rules.name: cache_after_measuring
rules.condition: Adding caching before profiling or before observing actual performance problems
rules.recommendation: Build without a cache first. Trust database buffer pools and indexes. If you need caching, start with in-process cache (Python lru_cache, Django local-memory, Caffeine for Java). Only add Redis/memcached when you have multiple servers sharing cache or data too large for process memory. Always add caching for specific measured hotspots, not everywhere.
dont_use: multi-layer caching (browser + CDN + Redis + in-app) for internal tools
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev's admin dashboard is a bit slow with 5 concurrent users. Rule should recommend adding database indexes first, not deploying Redis.

rules.name: direct_llm_api
rules.condition: Building an LLM app with < 3 sequential calls or straightforward prompt-response flows
rules.recommendation: Call LLM APIs directly using official SDKs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google). Use simple functions for chaining calls. Store prompts as version-controlled strings or templates. Implement conversation memory with a simple list. Only adopt orchestration frameworks when you need dynamic tool use, complex branching, or agent-level autonomy that you cannot easily code.
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev building a FAQ chatbot that does embedding lookup + one LLM call. Rule should recommend direct API calls, not LangChain.

rules.name: api_over_self_hosting
rules.condition: Needing LLM inference for a project without proven high-volume demand or strict data-residency requirements
rules.recommendation: Use hosted LLM APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) for inference — they handle scaling, optimization, and updates. For customization, try prompt engineering and RAG before fine-tuning. If fine-tuning is needed, use provider-hosted fine-tuning (OpenAI fine-tuning, LoRA) before full DIY training. Only self-host when usage volume justifies the GPU cost and ops burden.
dont_use: full ML pipelines with PyTorch (for classification tasks solvable by few-shot)
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev wants to customize a chatbot for their niche domain. Rule should recommend RAG + prompt engineering on GPT-4, not fine-tuning LLaMA-7B.

rules.name: single_agent_first
rules.condition: Building an AI agent or assistant with a single primary task or a well-defined workflow
rules.recommendation: Start with one well-scoped agent with a clear prompt and 2-5 tools max. For multi-step tasks, use a deterministic script with multiple LLM calls in sequence (you orchestrate the logic). Use a router pattern (classifier + specialized prompts) for diverse inputs. Limit tool count to < 15. Only add a second agent when a single agent demonstrably fails due to context window limits or truly independent parallel tasks.
dont_use: multi-agent orchestration frameworks (AutoGPT, CrewAI) for prototypes
agents invoking agents without clear justification
Hugging Face Transformers Agents (for basic tasks)
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev building a coding assistant that needs web search + code generation. Rule should recommend one agent with 2 tools, not a multi-agent swarm.

rules.name: skip_mlops_early
rules.condition: Doing prompt engineering or simple LLM integration (not training/retraining ML models at scale)
rules.recommendation: Track prompt versions in Git. Evaluate prompts with simple scripts or spreadsheets. Use basic logging (print/logging module) for LLM call monitoring. Only set up MLflow/Kubeflow/Weights&Biases when you are actually training models with hyperparameter sweeps, or when your prompt experiments exceed what Git + simple scripts can track.
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev iterating on prompts for a content generator asks about experiment tracking. Rule should recommend Git + evaluation scripts, not MLflow.

rules.name: skip_feature_store
rules.condition: Running a single model or LLM app with simple input data that doesn't require shared feature computation across teams
rules.recommendation: Pass features directly in code or store them in your existing database. Feature stores solve a cross-team, multi-model coordination problem — if you have one model and one developer, the overhead is pure waste. Only adopt a feature store when multiple models consume the same computed features and you need point-in-time consistency.
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev with one RAG pipeline asks whether to set up Feast for managing embeddings. Rule should recommend storing features in Postgres/files, not a feature store.

rules.name: minimal_observability
rules.condition: Running a small app with < 5 services on < 3 servers
rules.recommendation: Use structured logging to stdout/files + basic error alerting (email, PagerDuty free tier, or Sentry). Application-level health checks and simple dashboards (Grafana with your existing DB metrics) are enough. Only deploy full observability stacks (distributed tracing, metrics pipelines, log aggregation) when you have enough services and traffic that correlating issues across boundaries is a real debugging need.
dont_use: OpenTelemetry collector + Jaeger + Prometheus + Grafana stack (for one service)
Datadog/New Relic enterprise tiers (for hobby projects)
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev asks how to monitor their single FastAPI app. Rule should recommend Sentry + structured logging, not a full OpenTelemetry stack.

rules.name: simple_workflow_orchestration
rules.condition: Running sequential or simple parallel data processing tasks that can be expressed as a script
rules.recommendation: Use a Python script with asyncio, a cron job, or your framework's background task system. For simple ETL, a scheduled script is fine. Only adopt workflow engines when you have complex DAGs with retries, branching, human-in-the-loop steps, or multi-team coordination that simple scripts can't reliably handle.
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev needs to run a nightly data sync from 3 APIs into their database. Rule should recommend a cron + Python script, not Airflow.

rules.name: orm_with_eyes_open
rules.condition: Choosing a data access strategy for a new project
rules.recommendation: Use an ORM for rapid prototyping but monitor generated SQL from day one. Profile queries early (EXPLAIN ANALYZE). Drop to raw SQL or a query builder (Knex, Dapper) for hot paths and complex joins. Learn enough SQL to not depend entirely on the ORM. Watch for N+1 queries and ORM-generated bloat.
dont_use: blind ORM usage without query profiling
ORM for all queries including analytics and reporting
ORM-only approach when complex joins are needed
heavy ORMs (Hibernate) for simple CRUD apps where a query builder suffices
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev using Django ORM notices slow page loads at 6 months. Rule should recommend profiling with django-debug-toolbar and writing raw SQL for heavy queries, not blindly trusting the ORM.

rules.name: lightweight_frontend
rules.condition: Building a web UI for an MVP, internal tool, or content-oriented site
rules.recommendation: Start with the simplest frontend that handles current needs. For content sites: server-rendered (Rails + Hotwire, Next.js static export, Eleventy). For interactive apps: lightweight frameworks (Vue, Svelte) or React with minimal tooling. Use a UI kit (Tailwind, Material UI) instead of custom design systems. Progressively enhance — add complexity only when the simple approach breaks down.
dont_use: Angular (for small apps — steep learning curve, large bundles)
full SSR framework (for apps that could be client-rendered)
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev building an internal admin dashboard asks which frontend framework to use. Rule should recommend a lightweight option like Vue + Tailwind, not Angular.

rules.name: delay_state_management
rules.condition: Starting a new frontend app and considering global state management
rules.recommendation: Start with framework built-ins: React useState/useContext, Vue reactive/props. For server data, use React Query / TanStack Query instead of Redux for API caching. Only introduce global state management (Zustand, Jotai — not Redux) when prop drilling becomes a real problem across 3+ levels. Dan Abramov: 'Don't use Redux until you have problems with vanilla React.'
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev starting a React app asks whether to add Redux. Rule should recommend useState + useContext first, and React Query for server state.

rules.name: polling_before_websockets
rules.condition: Adding live-update features to an app where data changes infrequently (< 1 update/second per user)
rules.recommendation: Use periodic polling (every 10-30 seconds) or long-polling first. HTTP polling is simple, stateless, and works with standard infrastructure (CDNs, load balancers, proxies). Only adopt WebSockets when you need sub-second latency for truly real-time features (chat, collaborative editing, live gaming). SSE (Server-Sent Events) is a simpler middle ground for one-directional updates.
dont_use: Firebase Realtime Database (when periodic sync suffices)
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev building a dashboard with data that updates every 5 minutes asks about WebSockets. Rule should recommend polling every 30 seconds, not Socket.IO.

rules.name: managed_auth_over_custom
rules.condition: Implementing user authentication for a new web app as a solo developer
rules.recommendation: Use a managed auth service (Clerk, Auth0 free tier, Firebase Auth, Supabase Auth) or your framework's built-in auth module. These handle password hashing, token management, social logins, and security patches. Rolling your own auth is one of the highest-risk DIY decisions — one mistake means compromised user data. Only roll custom auth when you have regulatory requirements that managed services can't meet.
dont_use: storing passwords in plaintext or with weak hashing
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev asks whether to implement their own JWT auth or use a service. Rule should strongly recommend a managed auth service for security reasons.

rules.name: managed_infra
rules.condition: Solo developer deciding whether to self-host infrastructure (databases, servers, CI/CD) vs using managed services
rules.recommendation: Use managed services by default: managed databases (RDS, Supabase, PlanetScale), managed hosting (Vercel, Render, Fly.io), managed CI (GitHub Actions). Self-hosting means you're the DBA, sysadmin, and security team. Only self-host when cost at scale justifies it or when you have specific compliance/data-residency requirements.
dont_use: self-hosted PostgreSQL on bare metal (for MVPs)
self-hosted GitLab CI (when GitHub Actions exists)
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev asks whether to run their own database server or use a managed service. Rule should recommend managed (RDS/Supabase) to reduce ops burden.

rules.name: avoid_niche_tech
rules.condition: Choosing a technology stack and considering a cutting-edge or niche framework/language with a small community
rules.recommendation: Prefer boring, proven technology with large communities, abundant documentation, and easy hiring. PostgreSQL > exotic NewSQL. Python/JS/Go > niche languages. Django/FastAPI/Express > bleeding-edge frameworks. The cost of a niche choice compounds: fewer Stack Overflow answers, fewer libraries, harder to get help, harder to find contributors. Only choose niche tech when it solves a specific problem no mainstream tool can.
dont_use: brand-new frameworks with < 1 year of stability
programming languages with tiny communities (for primary stack)
databases without proven production track record
tools that require the creator's personal support to debug
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev considering a hot new Rust web framework with 500 GitHub stars for their SaaS backend. Rule should recommend a proven framework (FastAPI, Express, Django) instead.

rules.name: no_premature_abstractions
rules.condition: Writing code for the first implementation of a feature, especially with only one use case
rules.recommendation: Write concrete, specific code first. Don't create abstractions, interfaces, or 'flexible' architectures until you have 3+ concrete use cases that would benefit. Three similar lines of code are better than a premature abstraction. Avoid enterprise patterns (Factory, Strategy, AbstractVisitor) in small codebases. The Rule of Three: duplicate, then abstract.
dont_use: abstract base classes with one subclass
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev writes an AbstractDatabaseFactory with one PostgreSQL implementation. Rule should recommend removing the abstraction and using Postgres directly.

rules.name: tdd_where_it_matters
rules.condition: Deciding whether to use TDD for a new feature or module
rules.recommendation: Use TDD for: pure logic/algorithms, data transformations, API contracts, validation rules, bug fixes (write failing test first), and any code where correctness is critical. Skip strict TDD for: UI layout/styling, exploratory prototypes, one-off scripts, thin wrappers around external APIs. Minimum viable testing: unit tests for core logic, integration tests for API endpoints, manual smoke tests for UI.
dont_use: testing framework internals or third-party library behavior
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev asks whether to write tests first for a new pricing calculator function. Rule should recommend TDD (pure logic, correctness-critical). But for a CSS layout change, recommend manual verification.

rules.name: risk_based_testing
rules.condition: Planning test strategy for a solo project with limited time
rules.recommendation: Prioritize tests by risk and blast radius: (1) integration tests for critical paths (payment, auth, data mutations), (2) unit tests for complex business logic, (3) smoke tests for deployment verification. Skip tests for trivial getters/setters, UI-only components, and code that changes daily during active development. Test the 20% of code that causes 80% of bugs.
dont_use: comprehensive test suites for every function
separate test environments mirroring production exactly (for MVPs)
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev with limited time asks what to test first. Rule should recommend integration tests for payment flow and auth, not unit tests for utility helpers.

rules.name: scripted_deploys
rules.condition: Setting up CI/CD for a solo project or small team with < 5 repos
rules.recommendation: Start with GitHub Actions (or equivalent) for basic CI: lint, test, deploy on push to main. Use a simple deploy script (rsync, scp, or platform CLI). Only build multi-environment pipelines (dev/staging/prod) when you have actual users who would be impacted by untested changes. One environment (production) with feature flags is simpler than maintaining three environments.
dont_use: multi-stage Docker builds with matrix CI (for one service)
custom deployment orchestration (when a PaaS handles it)
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev asks whether to set up Jenkins with staging/prod pipeline for their side project. Rule should recommend GitHub Actions with deploy-on-push to one environment.

rules.name: nosql_only_when_needed
rules.condition: Choosing NoSQL (MongoDB, Firebase) as primary database for an app with relational data (users, posts, comments, orders)
rules.recommendation: If your data has relationships (users → posts → comments, orders → items), use SQL. NoSQL excels at: document-oriented data without joins, very high write throughput, or truly schemaless rapid iteration on non-relational data. Firebase is great for real-time sync but expensive at scale and limited in querying. MongoDB can work but you'll fight it for relational patterns. Default to PostgreSQL — it handles JSON, full-text search, and vector search too.
dont_use: MongoDB as primary DB for relational data
Firebase/Firestore for complex queries or analytics
DynamoDB without understanding its query limitations
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev building a social app (users, friends, posts, likes) asks whether to use MongoDB. Rule should recommend PostgreSQL since the data is relational.

rules.name: tdd_anti_patterns
rules.condition: Writing tests and noticing excessive mocking, brittle tests, or tests that mirror implementation
rules.recommendation: Test behavior, not implementation. Avoid mocking everything — use real dependencies where practical (real DB for integration tests, in-memory variants for unit tests). Don't test private methods directly. Don't write tests that break on every refactor (testing implementation details). One assertion per test concept. Keep test setup minimal and readable.
dont_use: mocking every dependency (leads to false confidence)
copy-pasting implementation logic into test assertions
test frameworks that require more code than the thing being tested
rules.test_scenario: Solo dev has tests that break every time they refactor, even though behavior is unchanged. Rule should recommend testing public API behavior, not internal implementation details.
